content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

August 23, 1996


Helen Fisicaro
490 D Street
Colma, CA  94014

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-96-230

Dear Ms. Fisicaro:

This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of
yourself, Donna Fisicaro, Phil Lum, and Pro Vallerga,
councilmembers of the City of Colma, regarding the "mass mailing"
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

QUESTION

If a city newsletter does not include a city councilmember's
photograph or signature, or reference the elected officer, may a
city newsletter include an article obtained from the Internet for
purposes of the "mass mailing" restrictions under the Act?

CONCLUSION

Provided the city newsletter does not make any reference to
the elected officer, "feature" him or her by including a
photograph or signature, or single out the elected officer by the
manner of display of his or her name or office in the layout of
the newsletter, the city newsletter may include the article in the
publication.

FACTS

"Christmas in April" is a national volunteer community-based
project dedicated to helping low-income, elderly, and disabled

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. Commission regulations
appear at Title 2, Sections 18000-18995 of the California Code of
Regulations.


428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814  (916) 322-5660

File No. A-96-230
Page 2


people by repairing and renovating their homes as well as
nonprofit facilities that benefit underprivileged populations.

This annual program brings volunteers and beneficiaries
together in the tradition of an old-fashioned "barn-raising." All
hands-on work takes place in April, with the project culminating
in an all-out, one-day effort by both skilled and non-skilled
volunteers to complete the renovations.

On April 27, 1996, the Mid-Peninsula Chapter of "Christmas in
April" program sponsored work on the home of Dorothy Larkin, a
resident of the Town of Colma. Prior to April 1996, and
continuing to the present, you, Dennis Fisicaro, Phil Lum, and Fro
Vallerga were members of the city council of the Town of Colma.
You and Fro Vallerga were the two Volunteer Coordinators who
coordinated the work at Mrs. Larkin's home. Dennis Fisicaro was
the House Captain for the Larkin home. He was in charge of
assigning volunteers to different crews, e.g., landscaping, clean­
up, painting, etc. Phil Lum was one of more than 40 volunteers
who worked on Mrs. Larkin's house.

The Town of Colma has a population of approximately 1,100
with about 500 registered voters. The town publishes a monthly
newsletter which is mailed to approximately 300 addresses in the
town, including some businesses. Typically, there are articles
about current matters of local interest, such as the award of a
bid for a recreation center; a log of police calls; a report of
actions taken at the last city council meeting; recognition of
residents for accomplishments, such as honor roll, sports awards,
new baby, etc; and the agenda for the next meeting.

At the request of Dorothy Larkin, you drafted a proposed
article for the newsletter, entitled "Christmas in April * Mid-
Peninsula Comes to Colma." The article generally describes the
work on Mrs. Larkin's home and names many of the volunteers who
worked there, including the city councilmembers who participated
in the program as volunteers. The article quotes Mrs. Larkin as
saying, "Even though I didn't know half of you that worked so hard
on Christmas in April, I want to say thank you to each and
everyone of you and especially those who put it all together."

The article contains a group photograph of the 40-plus
volunteers surrounding Mrs. Larkin in front of her house.
Included in the photograph are the four council members who worked
on Mrs. Larkin's home.

You believe that only those volunteers who worked on
Mrs. Larkin's home, and maybe those who attend city council
meetings (less than 100 people) would know the respective roles of
Fro Vallerga and yourself as the volunteer coordinators, of Dennis
Fisicaro as the House Captain, and of Phil Lum as one of the
volunteers without title.

File No. A-96-230
Page 3


ANALYSIS

Mass Mailings

Section 89001 prohibits mass mailings sent at public expense.
A "mass mailing" consists of over two hundred substantially
similar pieces of mail. (Section 82041.5.)2 A mass mailing is
"sent at public expense" if any of the cost of design, production,
printing or distribution, is paid for with public moneys.
(Regulation 18901(a).) For purposes of Section 89001, the city
newsletter constitutes a mass mailing sent at public expense
because the cost of design, production, printing and distribution
is paid for with public moneys.

Regulation 18901 interprets this provision and provides:

     (a) Except as provided in subdivision (b), a
mailing is prohibited by Section 89001 if all of
the following criteria are met:

     (1) Any item sent is delivered, by any means,
to the recipient at his or her residence, place of
employment or business, or post office box. For
purposes of this subdivision (a)(1), the item
delivered to the recipient must be a tangible item,
such as a videotape, record, or button, or a
written document.

     (2) The item sent either:

     (A) Features an elected officer affiliated
with the agency which produces or sends the
mailing, or

     (B) Includes the name, office, photograph, or
other reference to an elected officer affiliated
with the agency which produces or sends the
mailing, and is prepared or sent in cooperation,
consultation, coordination, or concert with the
elected officer.

     (3)(A) Any of the costs of distribution is
paid for with public moneys; or

     (B) Costs of design, production, and printing
exceeding $50.00 are paid with public moneys, and
the design, production, or printing is done with

_________________________

2 According to our telephone conversation of August 6, 1996, the
newsletter is distributed to more than 200 persons in any one
month.

File No. A-96-230
Page 4


the intent of sending the item other than as
permitted by this regulation.

(4) More than two hundred substantially
similar items are sent, in a single calendar month,
excluding any item sent in response to an
unsolicited request and any item described in
subdivision (b).

* * * *

(c) The following definitions shall govern
the interpretation of this regulation:

(1) "Elected officer affiliated with the
agency" means an elected officer who is a member,
officer, or employee of the agency, or of a subunit
thereof such as a committee, or who has supervisory
control over the agency, or who appoints one or
more members of the agency.

(2) "Features an elected officer" means that
the item mailed includes the elected officer's
photograph or signature, or singles out the elected
officer by the manner of display of his or her name
or office in the layout of the document, such as by
headlines, captions, type size, typeface, or type
color.

Councilmembers of the City of Colma are "affiliated" with the
entity that produces the newsletter. Therefore, they may not be
"featured" in the newsletter by use of their signatures,
photographs, or the manner of display of their names or offices in
the layout of the newsletter such as by headlines, type size or
typeface. (Regulation 18901(a)(2)(A).) Examples of the
Commission considers a public official to be "featured" in a
publication include: placing his or her name in headlines,
inclusion of his or her photograph, or by inclusion of an article
which is signed by the official.

The article that you have submitted to us contains a group
photograph, including some of the city councilmembers, and
numerous references to "volunteer coordinators" or "House
Captain." The House Captain identified is two of the volunteer coordinators
are councilmembers. Presumably, you would be identified as the
author of the article.

To comply with the "mass mailings" restrictions of Regulation
18901(a)(2)(A), the article cannot include a photograph that
includes the city councilmembers or the names or signatures of the
officials. Therefore, you could not be identified as the author
of the article. In our telephone conversation of August 6, 1996,
you asked whether you may block the city councilmembers out of the
picture or crop the picture to cut the officials out. This would

File No. A-96-230
Page 5


be permissible so long as the manner in which it is done does not
"feature" the official as described above.

     Even if the councilmembers are not featured within meaning of
Regulation 18901(a)(2)(A), if the mailing has been prepared or
sent in cooperation, consultation, coordination or concert with
the elected officers, in addition, any reference to the officers
is prohibited. (Regulation 18901(a)(2)(B).) Under the
circumstances you have described, the letter may not contain any
reference to the councilmembers unless an exception applies. No
exception included in Regulation 18901(b) appears to be
applicable.

     You have asked whether inclusion of terms such as "volunteer
coordinators" and "House Captain" are prohibited references to the
city councilmembers within meaning of Regulation 18901(a)(2)(B).

     To comply with Regulation 18901(a)(2)(B), you cannot be
identified by name or other information identifying your office,
then refer to yourself in other ways. For example, we have
advised that where an official was identified in his or her
official capacity in the text of an article, references by means
of the pronoun "I" were additional references to the official.
(Owen Advice Letter, No. I-90-704; Thorson Advice Letter,
No. 92-022.) Similarly, you could not identify the city
councilmembers by name or other information identifying their
office, then refer to them as either "volunteer coordinators" or
"House Captain." However, references to "volunteer coordinators"
or "House Captain" alone, without any other reference to the
councilmembers by name, office, or their official capacity, would
not be considered a reference to the elected officials for
purposes of the "mass mailing" prohibitions. Please note,
however, that a determination on this issue is necessarily a
factual determination that must be made on a case-by-case basis.

     If you have any questions regarding this matter, please feel
free to contact me at (916) 322-5660.


                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel
                                        [signature]
                                        By: Luisa Menchaca
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:LM:ak
